_source.name: EIA Residential Energy Consumption Survey 2020 (RECS 2020) + NREL ResStock Technical Reference Guide 2025
_source.publisher: U.S. Energy Information Administration / National Renewable Energy Laboratory
_source.units: wall/roof tier name (matches simulation_surface_materials enum), R-values in hr-ft^2-F/Btu
_source.methodology: Keyed by (census_region, vintage_bin). Tier names mirror the simulation_surface_materials enum: Standard / Insulated / Super Insulated. R-values follow ResStock TRG (which composites RECS 2020 envelope survey + NEEA RBSA 2012 + Home Innovation Research Labs 1982-2007 data + IECC minimums by vintage). Where ResStock TRG and RECS conflict, the IECC vintage minimum is used as a defensible compromise. Wall R-values are CAVITY only (does not include sheathing/finish layers); roof R-values are CEILING insulation only.

_notes: Vintage bins follow RECS published categorization: pre-1980 (built before 1980), 1980-1999, 2000-2009, 2010+ (built 2010 or later).

Tier-to-R-value mapping is fuzzy: 'Standard'~R-5 to R-7 wall / R-11 to R-19 roof; 'Insulated'~R-11 to R-13 wall / R-19 to R-30 roof; 'Super Insulated'~R-20+ wall / R-38+ roof. The tier name drives urbanopt's construction-set lookup; r_value can override.

For multifamily, RECS reports lower R-values on average for shared walls (party walls treated as adiabatic in urbanopt residential workflow). Single-family detached values used here as the conservative default.

window_to_wall_ratio_by_building_type: residential WWR is keyed by building_type (not region/vintage) because RECS reports the variance across regions/vintages as smaller than the dispersion across home types. SFD WWR median ~15% per ResStock TRG distributions. SFA slightly lower due to shorter exterior walls per dwelling unit. Multifamily aggregate covers both mid-rise and high-rise. IECC 2021 caps residential WWR at 25% in most climates.
